title: Existence theory of minimal hypersurfaces
speaker: Fernando Marquez
abstract: In this talk I will survey recent advances on the existence theory of minimal hypersurfaces from the variational point of view. I will discuss what we know, what we do not know and point to future directions. This is based on joint works with Andre Neves.

proof_stages.label: Variational setting and min--max motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the area functional on closed hypersurfaces in a closed Riemannian manifold and motivates min--max methods through the classical closed-geodesic problem, where Birkhoff's sweepouts replace unsuccessful direct minimization.

proof_stages.label: Almgren--Pitts flat-cycle existence theorem
proof_stages.summary: Parametrized surfaces are replaced by flat cycles, one-parameter sweepouts define a width, and the Almgren--Pitts--Schoen--Simon theorem realizes this width by an embedded minimal hypersurface, smooth in ambient dimensions three through seven.

proof_stages.label: Mod-2 cycle-space topology and k-widths
proof_stages.summary: Working with mod-2 nullhomologous cycles makes the cycle space weakly homotopy equivalent to RP-infinity, whose fundamental cohomology class defines k-sweepouts and the volume spectrum omega_k.

proof_stages.label: Sublinear-growth contradiction for the no-stable case
proof_stages.summary: The Marques--Neves argument gives either infinitely many minimal hypersurfaces or a disjoint stable obstruction; when no stable hypersurface exists, each width is a single multiple, and finitely many hypersurfaces would force linear growth contradicting the Gromov--Guth sublinear bounds.

proof_stages.label: Weyl law and generic density
proof_stages.summary: The Liokumovich--Marques--Neves Weyl law gives the asymptotic growth of the volume spectrum, and together with min--max realization plus index estimates it feeds Irie's perturbation argument proving that, for a Baire-generic metric, the union of minimal hypersurfaces is dense.

proof_stages.label: Core-and-cylinder argument for arbitrary metrics
proof_stages.summary: Song's proof for general metrics cuts along stable hypersurfaces to a minimal core with the Frankel property, studies the volume spectrum of its cylindrical extension, and uses a controlled linear asymptotic plus a counting argument to obtain infinitely many minimal hypersurfaces.

proof_stages.label: Multiplicity-one and Morse-index program via Allen--Cahn
proof_stages.summary: For generic metrics the conjectural picture is that omega_k is realized with multiplicity one and Morse index k; multiplicity one is known to imply the index statement, and in dimension three Allen--Cahn min--max compactness yields this multiplicity-one/index result.

examples.name: Poincare--Birkhoff closed geodesic on a Riemannian sphere
examples.note: The one-dimensional closed-geodesic problem, especially Birkhoff's sweepout of a sphere by loops, is used to show why minimization fails and to generate the min--max idea used later.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explains Birkhoff's minimax construction on a Riemannian sphere, using loop sweepouts and a sup-inf length to obtain a closed geodesic.

passes.reason: The Birkhoff geodesic realizes a positive min-max length/width rather than refuting a claim.
the Birkhoff geodesic is a nontrivial min–max/length-critical loop used to prove existence, not to refute a claim
An ordinary min-max closed geodesic on an arbitrary Riemannian sphere is used to motivate the variational method, not to refute a claim.
It is the Birkhoff minimax closed geodesic realizing a positive length width on the sphere.
It is the nontrivial min-max critical closed geodesic realizing Birkhoff’s width, used for existence.
it is the nontrivial Birkhoff min–max geodesic realizing a sweepout width on an otherwise arbitrary Riemannian sphere
It is used as the min--max geodesic realizing Birkhoff's width on a Riemannian sphere, not to refute a claim.
it is Birkhoff’s nontrivial minimax geodesic realizing a width on a Riemannian sphere, not a refuting example
Birkhoff’s geodesic is used as a positive min–max existence example, and it is a length-critical/min–max loop rather than a symmetric or sporadic object.
it is the nontrivial Birkhoff min–max critical geodesic of the length functional, used to prove existence rather than refute a claim
it is a nontrivial length-critical geodesic realizing a minimax value, used to illustrate existence
Birkhoff’s nontrivial closed geodesic is a min–max critical loop realizing the one-parameter width.

examples.name: Small handle or spike in the flat topology
examples.note: A sphere with a tiny handle or spike illustrates that flat closeness measures small enclosed volume rather than topological similarity.
examples.engagement_reason: The small handle and spike are used only as visual illustrations of flat-topology closeness via small enclosed volume, with no detailed calculation or construction carried out.

passes.reason: A vanishingly small handle or spike is a collapsed flat-topology feature used to show flat closeness need not preserve topology or smooth shape.

examples.name: Level sets of a Morse function as a sweepout
examples.note: Choosing a Morse function and taking its level sets makes the abstract one-parameter sweepout definition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: Level sets of a chosen Morse function are simply given as an obvious example of a sweepout.

passes.reason: Morse level sets give a standard representative sweepout construction.
Morse level sets give a standard representative sweepout rather than a special or adversarial object

examples.name: Spherical harmonics for estimating the Weyl-law constant
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions using spherical harmonics on a sphere to obtain upper and lower estimates for the universal constant in the volume-spectrum Weyl law.
examples.engagement_reason: Spherical harmonics are only cited as a possible way to estimate the Weyl-law constant, without performing the estimate.

passes.reason: Spherical harmonics are the rotation-adapted eigenspaces on the round sphere used as an explicit estimating model.
spherical harmonics form the highly regular symmetry-adapted eigenfunction family on the sphere and are used only for estimating the constant
Spherical harmonics come from the highly regular round sphere and are used as a symmetric model for estimating the Weyl-law constant.
Spherical harmonics form the highly regular rotation-equivariant spectral model on the round sphere used for estimates.
Spherical harmonics are Laplacian eigenfunctions on the highly symmetric round sphere, used as test objects for estimates.
spherical harmonics form the highly symmetric eigenfunction families on the round sphere used for estimates
Spherical harmonics are invoked as the highly regular round-sphere eigenfunction family for estimating constants, not as an obstruction.
spherical harmonics are highly regular rotation-theoretic eigenfunction families on the round sphere used for estimates
Spherical harmonics are rotation-adapted Laplacian eigenfunctions on the round sphere, hence symmetric and Rayleigh-critical, and are used only for estimates.
spherical harmonics are Laplace-eigenfunction/Rayleigh-critical families on the highly symmetric round sphere, used only as model estimates
spherical harmonics are the highly regular modes on the maximally symmetric sphere, used for estimates
Spherical harmonics form the highly regular SO(n+1)-symmetric spectral model used for estimates.

examples.name: Four minimal hypersurfaces and a metric bump in a disjoint ball
examples.note: The schematic case of only four minimal hypersurfaces and a local metric perturbation displays the key mechanism forcing creation of a new hypersurface crossing the chosen ball.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker runs through the perturbation argument with four minimal hypersurfaces, a disjoint ball, a metric bump, the Weyl-law change in widths, and the resulting creation of a new surface crossing the ball.

passes.reason: The minimal hypersurfaces are area-critical objects, and the finite disjoint-ball configuration is used as the adversarial setup contradicted by the metric bump/Weyl-law argument.
the finite collection of area-critical minimal hypersurfaces with a disjoint metric bump is the non-dense enemy configuration contradicted by the perturbation argument
The finite list of four hypersurfaces plus a local metric bump is an adversarial perturbation showing such a list cannot remain complete under the Weyl-law argument.
The finite collection avoiding a bumped ball is the adversarial hypothetical case contradicted to force a new minimal hypersurface.
The hypersurfaces are area-critical, and the disjoint metric bump is an enemy perturbation against the finite/no-surface-in-the-ball scenario.
the finite four-surface configuration is a hypothetical enemy case contradicted by bumping the metric and applying the Weyl law
The finite set of four minimal hypersurfaces avoiding a bumped ball is the adversarial case in the perturbation argument forcing a new surface.
the finite collection avoiding the bumped ball is the contradiction or enemy scenario in the density argument, while structurally it is otherwise ordinary
The finite collection avoiding a bumped ball is the enemy case in the perturbation contradiction, while the hypersurfaces themselves are area-critical minimal ones.
the finite list of area-critical minimal hypersurfaces is the adversarial configuration that a disjoint volume bump forces to fail
the finite collection of area-critical minimal hypersurfaces is an enemy case defeated by the metric bump argument
The finite collection with a disjoint ball is the hypothetical enemy case contradicted by the metric-bump/Weyl-law argument.

examples.name: Convex energy surface and Viterbo-type action bound
examples.note: An audience question invokes the symplectic example of bounding the least periodic-orbit action on a convex energy surface by a volume quantity as an analogy for possible area-volume estimates.
examples.engagement_reason: The convex energy surface and Viterbo-type action bound appear only as an audience-invoked analogy/conjectural comparison, not as an example worked through in the talk.

passes.reason: The Viterbo-type example concerns the least-action periodic orbit/capacity of a convex energy surface as an extremal bound, not as a disproof.
